g.data-narration: Servlets are Java programs running on web servers that handle client requests between browsers and backend databases.
Servlets extend server capabilities, handle HTTP requests, generate dynamic responses, and are platform-independent.
The servlet lifecycle has three phases: init() for one-time setup, service() handling each request, and destroy() for cleanup.
Jakarta EE renamed javax.servlet to jakarta.servlet. Key packages: jakarta.servlet (core) and jakarta.servlet.http (HTTP).
Core interfaces include Servlet, ServletConfig, ServletContext, Request, and Response. GenericServlet provides base implementation.
g.text: Most servlets extend HttpServlet for HTTP protocol support

g.data-narration: HttpServletRequest provides getParameter(), getAttribute(), getMethod(). HttpServletResponse handles content type and output streams.
HTTP is stateless. Servlets provide four session tracking methods: Cookies, URL Rewriting, Hidden Fields, and HttpSession.
The web.xml deployment descriptor configures servlets, URL mappings, session timeout, and other application settings.
g.text: Modern approach: @WebServlet annotation (replaces XML in Jakarta EE)

g.data-narration: Servlets outperform CGI with thread-based processing, platform independence, shared memory, and better scalability.
In summary, the Servlet Package provides jakarta.servlet and jakarta.servlet.http APIs for building Java web applications with request handling, session management, and deployment configuration.
Remember: init() runs once, service() handles each request, javax became jakarta in Jakarta EE 9+, servlets use threads not processes.
g.text: 💡 Exam Tips: Lifecycle methods | Package rename | Thread safety | doGet/doPost
